title: Real Estate Investing with a Self-Directed IRA: The Complete Guide
excerpt: A self-directed IRA lets you invest retirement dollars directly into real estate — tax-free or tax-deferred. Learn the rules, benefits, and step-by-step process to get started.
author: AIWholesail Team
tags: real estate investing with self directed IRA
metaDescription: Learn how real estate investing with a self-directed IRA works, including rules, tax benefits, and steps to buy your first property with retirement funds.
metaKeywords: real estate investing with self directed IRA, self directed IRA real estate, SDIRA investing, buy rental property with IRA, tax-free real estate investing, self directed IRA rules, SDIRA custodian

sections.content: Most people know you can invest retirement money in stocks, bonds, and mutual funds. But far fewer know you can also use a self-directed IRA to buy real estate — rental properties, fix-and-flips, raw land, and even tax liens. Real estate investing with a self-directed IRA is one of the most powerful wealth-building strategies available to everyday investors, yet it remains one of the most underused. If you're already building a rental portfolio — or thinking about starting one — this guide will show you exactly how to combine the power of real estate with the tax advantages of a retirement account. For a broader look at building long-term wealth through rentals, check out our [Rental Property Investing for Beginners: Complete Guide](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/rental-property-investing-guide).
What Is a Self-Directed IRA?

A self-directed IRA (SDIRA) is a type of individual retirement account that allows you to invest in a much wider range of assets than a traditional brokerage IRA. While a standard IRA at Fidelity or Vanguard limits you to stocks and funds, a self-directed IRA lets you hold physical real estate, private loans, precious metals, and more — all within the same tax-advantaged wrapper. The key difference is the custodian. SDIRAs must be held with a specialized custodian that permits alternative assets. Popular options include Equity Trust, uDirect IRA, and Alto IRA. These companies handle the account administration, but you — the investor — make all the investment decisions.
Traditional vs. Roth Self-Directed IRA

You can open a self-directed version of either a Traditional or Roth IRA. With a Traditional SDIRA, contributions may be tax-deductible, and all growth is tax-deferred — meaning you pay taxes when you withdraw funds in retirement. With a Roth SDIRA, contributions are made with after-tax dollars, but all growth and qualified withdrawals in retirement are completely tax-free. For real estate investors who expect significant appreciation and rental income, the Roth SDIRA is often the more powerful long-term choice. Imagine buying a rental property that doubles in value over 15 years — inside a Roth IRA, all of that gain is yours tax-free.
The Key Rules You Must Know

The IRS allows real estate inside an IRA, but it comes with strict rules. Breaking these rules can result in the entire account being treated as a taxable distribution — a potentially devastating mistake. Before you invest, understand these non-negotiables.

items: No personal use: You cannot live in, vacation in, or personally use any property held in your SDIRA — even for a single night.
No disqualified persons: You cannot buy from or sell to yourself, your spouse, parents, children, or certain business partners. These are called 'disqualified persons.'
All expenses must be paid by the IRA: Property taxes, repairs, insurance, and management fees must all flow through the IRA account — not your personal bank account.
All income goes back to the IRA: Rent checks must be deposited directly into the SDIRA, not your personal accounts.
No self-dealing: You cannot personally perform labor on the property (like renovations) and have the IRA pay you for it.
UBIT tax may apply: If your IRA uses a mortgage (called a non-recourse loan) to buy property, a portion of income may be subject to Unrelated Business Income Tax (UBIT).

sections.content: Pro Tip: To keep your SDIRA compliant, open a dedicated checking account in the name of your IRA for all property-related transactions. Your custodian can help you set this up correctly. Never commingle SDIRA funds with personal money — even temporarily.
Step-by-Step: How to Buy Real Estate with a Self-Directed IRA
Step 1: Open and Fund Your SDIRA

Start by choosing a reputable SDIRA custodian that specializes in real estate. Expect setup fees ranging from $50 to $300, plus annual maintenance fees of $200 to $500 or more depending on your account balance. You can fund the account through a rollover from an existing 401(k) or IRA, or by making new annual contributions (up to $7,000 in 2025, or $8,000 if you're 50 or older).
Step 2: Find the Right Investment Property

Not every deal makes sense inside an SDIRA. Because the cash flow and appreciation stay locked in the account until retirement, you want properties with strong fundamentals — consistent rental demand, low vacancy risk, and solid long-term appreciation. Markets like [Atlanta, GA](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/atlanta-ga), [Dallas, TX](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/dallas-tx), and [Phoenix, AZ](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/phoenix-az) are popular choices for SDIRA investors because of their population growth, landlord-friendly laws, and strong rental demand. Before making any offer, run the numbers carefully. Use our free [Deal Calculator](https://aiwholesail.com/tools/wholesale-deal-calculator) to quickly evaluate potential cash flow, returns, and purchase price thresholds. You should also know whether the property passes the [1% Rule](https://aiwholesail.com/blog/one-percent-rule-real-estate) — a quick screen that helps filter out low-yield deals.
Step 3: Direct Your Custodian to Purchase

This is where the SDIRA process differs most from a regular purchase. You cannot sign the purchase contract personally — the IRA must be the buyer of record. The title will read something like 'Equity Trust Company FBO [Your Name] IRA.' Your custodian will review the deal, handle the wire transfer from your IRA funds, and execute the closing paperwork on behalf of the account. This process can take 1 to 3 weeks longer than a standard cash closing, so factor that into your offer timeline.
Step 4: Manage the Property Through the IRA

Once you own the property inside your SDIRA, every dollar in and out must flow through the IRA. Since you can't personally manage the property without risking self-dealing violations, most SDIRA real estate investors hire a professional property manager. While this adds cost — typically 8% to 12% of monthly rent — it keeps your account compliant and your stress low. If you're weighing that cost, read our breakdown of [Property Management vs. Self-Managing](https://aiwholesail.com/blog/property-management-vs-self-managing) to understand exactly what you're getting for that fee.

'The two most powerful forces in the universe are compound interest and tax-deferred growth. A self-directed IRA lets real estate investors harness both at the same time.' — Common wisdom among SDIRA educators and retirement planning specialists
What Types of Real Estate Work Best in an SDIRA?

items: Single-family rentals: The most common SDIRA real estate investment. Easier to manage and finance with non-recourse loans.
Small multifamily (2-4 units): Higher cash flow potential, though requires more capital upfront.
Raw land: Simpler to hold (no tenants or repairs), though no rental income is generated.
Tax lien certificates: A more passive strategy where the IRA earns interest on overdue property taxes paid on behalf of homeowners.
Private lending / notes: Your IRA acts as the bank, earning interest income from other real estate investors — fully within IRA rules.
Fix-and-flip (with caution): Possible, but requires significant available IRA cash for both purchase and renovation, and all contractors must be arms-length third parties.

sections.content: Running the Numbers: A Quick Example

Let's say you roll over $120,000 from an old 401(k) into a Roth SDIRA. You use $100,000 to purchase a single-family rental in a growing market like Dallas. The home rents for $1,200 per month. After property management (10%), taxes, insurance, and reserves, your net cash flow is roughly $500 per month — or $6,000 per year. That's a 6% cash-on-cash return. Over 20 years, that $100,000 property could grow to $200,000 or more in value, while generating $120,000 in cumulative rent — all tax-free inside a Roth IRA. For a faster way to stress-test deals like this one, our guide on [How to Analyze a Rental Property in 10 Minutes](https://aiwholesail.com/blog/analyze-rental-property-10-minutes) walks you through the exact numbers you need to check before making an offer.
Common Mistakes to Avoid

items: Using personal funds for any IRA property expense — even a small repair — can trigger a prohibited transaction.
Buying property from or selling to a family member, even at fair market value, violates IRS rules.
Not budgeting enough reserves inside the IRA. If the roof needs replacing, the money must come from the IRA — not your wallet.
Choosing a custodian solely on price. Slow custodians can cost you deals. Ask about their average closing timeline before you sign up.
Ignoring UBIT. If you use a non-recourse mortgage, talk to a tax professional before you close to understand your tax exposure.

sections.content: Pro Tip: Before you purchase any property inside an SDIRA, have both a specialized SDIRA custodian AND a tax professional review the deal. The compliance cost of getting it right upfront is a fraction of the cost of an IRS audit or early distribution penalty.

Real estate investing with a self-directed IRA isn't the right fit for every investor, but for those with significant retirement savings and a long time horizon, it's one of the most tax-efficient ways to build real estate wealth. If you're newer to real estate investing overall, start with our [Real Estate Investing for Beginners](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/real-estate-investing-for-beginners) guide before diving into SDIRAs. And if you're interested in finding deals at below-market prices to buy with your IRA funds, our [Wholesaling Real Estate for Beginners](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/wholesaling-real-estate-for-beginners) guide explains how wholesalers find and assign deeply discounted properties — exactly the kind of deals that perform best inside a retirement account.
